title: Flat6Labs, IFC launch StartAlgeria capacity-building programme
summary: Egypt's Flat6Labs, the International Finance Corporation (IFC), and Algeria's Ministry of Knowledge Economy, Startups and Micro-Enterprises have launched StartAlgeria, a capacity-building programme targeting entrepreneur support organisations (ESOs) in Algeria. The programme aims to strengthen ESOs' ability to support pre-seed and seed-stage startups through workshops, masterclasses, and six months of post-programme mentorship, focusing on startup selection, program design, investment readiness, fundraising strategy, and financial sustainability. The pilot cohort will focus on incubators in Algiers.
content: Egypt's Flat6Labs, the IFC and Algeria's Ministry of Knowledge Economy, Startups and Micro-Enterprises have launched StartAlgeria, a capacity-building programme that puts entrepreneur support organisations (ESOs) at the forefront of Algeria's ecosystem future. StartAlgeria comes at a key moment for Algeria's entrepreneurship landscape, shifting the focus toward improving how the ESOs operate by providing them with international best practices adapted to each organization's needs, a community-driven approach that focuses on peer learning, and facilitating connections with investors, policymakers, and key stakeholders. The programme is designed to equip Algerian ESOs reinforcing pre-seed and seed-stage startups with the expertise, frameworks, and networks needed to contribute to a stronger, more competitive entrepreneurship ecosystem in Algeria and expand into global markets. StartAlgeria will pilot a first cohort focusing on incubators in the capital, Algiers. Following a call for application, the selected ESOs will go through a structured programme comprising workshops and masterclasses covering key areas such as startup selection, program design and delivery, and investment readiness. In addition to the core programme, participating ESOs will benefit from six months of post-programme mentorship, focusing on areas such as fundraising strategy, partnership development, financial sustainability, and programme improvement. This sustained engagement's goal is to provide a lasting impact in how Algerian ESOs operate and what they're able to offer the startups they champion. "Algeria's startup ecosystem is demonstrating remarkable potential and a rapidly growing level of maturity, driven by an ambitious new generation of founders, increasing institutional support, and a strong national commitment to innovation and entrepreneurship," said Yehia Houry, CEO of Flat6Labs. "The opportunity today lies in further empowering entrepreneurship support organisations to match this momentum by strengthening their ability to identify and nurture high-potential startups, deliver impactful and results-driven programs, and create stronger connections between entrepreneurs and sources of capital. With the right support structures in place, Algeria is well positioned to become one of the leading innovation hubs in the region."

In addition to the core programme, participating ESOs will benefit from six months of post-programme mentorship, focusing on areas such as fundraising strategy, partnership development, financial sustainability, and programme improvement. This sustained engagement's goal is to provide a lasting impact in how Algerian ESOs operate and what they're able to offer the startups they champion. "Algeria's startup ecosystem is demonstrating remarkable potential and a rapidly growing level of maturity, driven by an ambitious new generation of founders, increasing institutional support, and a strong national commitment to innovation and entrepreneurship," said Yehia Houry, CEO of Flat6Labs. "The opportunity today lies in further empowering entrepreneurship support organisations to match this momentum by strengthening their ability to identify and nurture high-potential startups, deliver impactful and results-driven programs, and create stronger connections between entrepreneurs and sources of capital. With the right support structures in place, Algeria is well positioned to become one of the leading innovation hubs in the region." title: Katapult Africa opens applications for latest climate-focused accelerator programme
summary: Katapult Africa has opened applications for its three-month climate-tech accelerator programme, offering equity investment of $150,000-$500,000, workshops, mentorship, and investor access to early-stage African startups in agritech, climate-smart agriculture, and resilient food systems.
content: Applications are open for the Katapult Africa accelerator programme, which is designed to support climate-tech startups in reaching key milestones – growth, investor readiness, and impact management. The three-month Katapult Africa programme gives participants access to intensive workshops, learning sessions, and a broad network of key stakeholders, including investors and industry experts. This equips them with the tools, knowledge, and connections needed to scale their impact across the continent. Participating ventures will also receive between US$150,000 and US$500,000 in equity investment. Katapult Africa is looking for impact-driven early-stage climate-tech startups operating in Africa, led by experienced teams, with strong paths to commercialisation or early revenues, and scalable business models. The focus is pan-African, across the following impact areas – agritech, climate-smart agriculture, and resilient food systems.

title: African tech startups raise $260m in Q2 2026; down 40% on same period last year
summary: Thirty-eight African tech startups raised $260 million in Q2 2026, a 40% decline from $427 million in Q2 2025. The annual African Tech Startups Funding Report by Disrupt Africa indicates a potential funding dip for 2026, with projected annual total of $1.28 billion, down from $1.64 billion in 2025.
content: Thirty-eight African tech startups raised a total of US$260 million in the second quarter of 2026, down 40 per cent on the US$427 million raised in the same period of 2025. Total investment into the African tech startup ecosystem increased by almost 50 per cent to US$1.64 billion in 2025 as the sector began to slowly recover from the impacts of the global capital shortage, according to the 11th edition of the annual African Tech Startups Funding Report released by startup news and research portal Disrupt Africa. Numbers from Q1 2026 had suggested this year would at least match, and probably surpass, 2025, with 40 startups banking US$382,150,000 in funding. Yet in Q2, 38 startups raised just $260 million, substantially down from the same period last year, and raising concerns African tech could be in for another funding dip. Extrapolated over the course of the year, that would be 156 startups (down from 178 in 2025), and an annual 2026 total of US$1.28 billion, down from US$1.6 billion in 2025.

title: Beltone, Telda partner to expand digital investing access in Egypt
summary: Beltone Asset Management, a subsidiary of Beltone Holding, has partnered with Egyptian fintech Telda to offer Beltone's investment products and mutual funds through Telda's digital platform. The collaboration provides Telda users with zero-commission access to funds such as Meya Meya, Sabayek, B-Secure, and Wafra EGX 33, aiming to enhance financial inclusion in Egypt.
content: Beltone Asset Management, a subsidiary of Beltone Holding, has partnered with Egyptian fintech company Telda, which specialises in digital financial services, to broaden access to Beltone's investment products and mutual funds through Telda's digital platform. The partnership aims to simplify access to investment solutions by enabling Telda users to invest in funds established and managed by Beltone Asset Management through a seamless digital experience that offers zero commission fees, excluding precious metals funds, with the ability to transfer redeemed proceeds directly to the Telda card, supporting broader financial inclusion and expanding participation in investment products across Egypt. By combining Beltone Asset Management's institutional expertise and established track record in asset management with Telda's user-centric digital infrastructure, the collaboration delivers streamlined access to a diversified suite of investment solutions through a fully integrated platform. Under the partnership, Telda users will gain access to a broad range of funds established and managed by Beltone Asset Management, including the "Meya Meya" fund, the "Sabayek" gold investment fund, "B-Secure," which offers a high level of liquidity, as well as "Wafra EGX 33," the Shariah-compliant equity fund, alongside a selection of sector-focused investment funds. "This partnership marks an important step toward delivering a more integrated financial experience for our users by bringing together everyday financial services and investment solutions within a single platform, enabling our users to access trusted investment products through a simple and flexible digital experience," said Ahmed Sabbah, CEO of Telda.

title: SA's Supascale launches Africa's first GPU cloud marketplace
summary: South African startup Supascale has launched Africa's first GPU cloud marketplace, making advanced AI models more affordable while allowing GPU owners to earn passive income.
content: South African startup Supascale has launched Africa's first GPU cloud marketplace, making advanced AI models more affordable while allowing GPU owners to earn passive income. Founded by Aaron Bornmann, Supascale enables users to access leading AI models at significantly lower costs, while having the world's fastest GPU onboarding process for GPU owners to monetise unused computing power through a cloud AI marketplace. As demand for AI continues to surge globally, and AI data centres are putting increasing strain on the environment through excessive noise, electricity and water resources usage for cooling, Supascale offers a green-alternative to accessing AI models powered by idle GPUs from laptops, PCs and unused GPUs in data centres – allowing everyday people to earn from the AI boom. “The AI economy needs to be accessible to everyone, not just large hyperscalers,” said Bornmann. “We're building the infrastructure that allows businesses, developers, creators, and innovators to access world-class AI capabilities while enabling everyday GPU owners to participate directly in the AI economy.” The company plans to expand its footprint internationally while positioning Africa as an active participant in the rapidly growing global AI infrastructure market.

title: Nigeria's MyAgent launches to build trust level for African real estate
summary: Nigerian prop-tech startup MyAgent has launched a platform connecting renters directly with verified landlords, aiming to build trust and transparency in Africa's real estate market, starting with Nigeria's rental sector. Founded by Samuel Afolabi, the startup is currently founder-funded, generating revenue through a 20% commission on rental transactions, and is now fundraising to accelerate supply-side growth.
content: Nigerian startup MyAgent has launched a prop-tech platform it says is building the trust and transaction layer for real estate in Africa, starting with Nigeria's rental market. Launched earlier this year by Samuel Afolabi, MyAgent connects renters directly with verified landlords, reduces dependence on agents, and makes the rental process faster, more transparent, and less expensive. "The long-term vision is to become the default infrastructure for property transactions, covering rentals first, then land, sales, shortlets, and eventually the data layer for the market," Afolabi told Disrupt Africa. Most real estate platforms in Nigeria focus on listings, but the real problem is trust and supply. "Renters want better access, but landlords are the hard side of the marketplace to win. Most platforms underestimate how important it is to actively acquire and verify supply, not just attract demand," Afolabi said. Though MyAgent's competition includes traditional agents, listing platforms, and rental marketplaces, it believes the winning model is the one that solves landlord acquisition, verification, and trust first. Currently founder-funded, the startup has already launched its product, onboarded landlords and renters, completed transactions, and is generating initial revenue. "We are now fundraising to accelerate growth, especially on the supply side," Afolabi said. MyAgent's immediate focus is building density and trust locally, before expanding. "Long-term, we plan to grow into property sales, land, and shortlets, and eventually expand into other African markets where the same problem exists," he said. The startup makes money through a 20 per cent commission on successful rental transactions.

title: SA's stub is an AI-native accounting platform built for everyday entrepreneurs
summary: South African startup stub has built an AI-native accounting platform designed for micro-entrepreneurs and small businesses, offering simple tools for invoicing, quotes, payments, and cashflow management with a full double-entry ledger. Founded in 2023, stub has partnered with iKhokha, Capitec, and Yoco to distribute its platform, now supporting nearly 10,000 businesses.
content: South African startup stub has built an AI-native accounting platform “built for everyday entrepreneurs”, doing all the things a small business needs, wrapped in something simple and intelligent. Founded in 2023, stub offers easy-to-use tools for sending invoices, managing quotes, tracking payments, and monitoring cashflow. Underneath that simplicity is a full double-entry ledger and customisable chart of accounts, so the books are real and compliant. Increasingly, the platform uses automation and intelligent data models to categorise transactions, reconcile against linked bank feeds and surface insights — automating the manual work so the business owner spends time on their business rather than on paperwork and everyday admin. The founding insight was personal, said Tayla Dandridge, co-founder and CEO of stub. “Having spent most of my career in financial services focused on small and micro businesses, I kept seeing the same thing — accounting tools were too complicated, too expensive, and too far removed from how people actually run a business day to day,” she told Disrupt Africa. “Traditional accounting platforms are generally built for accountants and established SMEs. They are powerful, but often too complex, time-consuming and costly for a tuck shop, a salon, freelancer or a side hustle.” The real “competition” for most of stub’s market isn’t another platform at all, Dandridge said. “It’s a spreadsheet, a WhatsApp chat, or a shoebox full of slips. That is the gap we spotted at the bottom of the pyramid: millions of micro-entrepreneurs and small business owners who were completely unserved by traditional accounting software,” she said. The startup, which bootstrapped a large portion of its journey until it found strategic angel investors, has secured a few major partnerships, which Dandridge says matter most. “iKhokha was our first big partnership – stub powers their iK Accounting product, and that single anchor relationship has driven a significant number of businesses onto our platform,” she said. “The second was our direct integration with Capitec, South Africa’s largest bank by customer numbers, which puts simple accounting in the hands of a huge base of entrepreneurs and side-hustlers. And most recently, our direct app integration with Yoco, the payment platform for independent business owners across the country.” Going from a single anchor partner, to a direct bank integration, to now working with Yoco in roughly 18 months are milestones Dandridge said she is proud of. “But the most exciting part is the access this creates for entrepreneurs who have never had this level of tooling to run their businesses,” she said. As a result, access has been strong and accelerating. “We’re now supporting just under 10,000 businesses on the stub platform, up from under 4,000 entrepreneurs roughly seven months ago. Signups have compounded every quarter – from a few hundred in 2023 to over 1,800 in a single quarter this year,” said Dandridge. stub’s strongest concentrations are Cape Town, Johannesburg, Pretoria and Durban, and its customer base skews toward micro and informal businesses like tuck shops, spazas, taverns, fast food outlets, salons, cafés, independent consultants and service-based entrepreneurs. “Our name is a statement of intent: stub is built for entrepreneurs. Platforms make global expansion a simpler task, and the playbook stays the same — simple tools distributed through partners and our own direct-to-consumer, on-the-ground channels,” Dandridge said. The startup runs off a subscription model. Businesses use the core tools for free and upgrade to paid plans for the fuller feature set, with payments revenue layered on through payment links. Dandridge said stub has had to overcome plenty of challenges, but has learnt that difficulties usually carry an opportunity inside them. “An unhappy customer is often one of the best teachers; almost every time, they are pointing at something that makes the product better for everyone else. And there’s also a handful of hard “no”s along the way – and more often than not, that pushes us toward something exciting just around the corner,” she said. “So, we try not to treat the hard moments as setbacks. They’ve shaped the product, the team, and how we show up for entrepreneurs – who, after all, deal with this kind of stuff every single day.”
document: Titre: SA's stub is an AI-native accounting platform built for everyday entrepreneurs Résumé: South African startup stub has built an AI-native accounting platform designed for micro-entrepreneurs and small businesses, offering simple tools for invoicing, quotes, payments, and cashflow management with a full double-entry ledger. Founded in 2023, stub has partnered with iKhokha, Capitec, and Yoco to distribute its platform, now supporting nearly 10,000 businesses. Contenu: South African startup stub has built an AI-native accounting platform “built for everyday entrepreneurs”, doing all the things a small business needs, wrapped in something simple and intelligent. Founded in 2023, stub offers easy-to-use tools for sending invoices, managing quotes, tracking payments, and monitoring cashflow. Underneath that simplicity is a full double-entry ledger and customisable chart of accounts, so the books are real and compliant. Increasingly, the platform uses automation and intelligent data models to categorise transactions, reconcile against linked bank feeds and surface insights — automating the manual work so the business owner spends time on their business rather than on paperwork and everyday admin. The founding insight was personal, said Tayla Dandridge, co-founder and CEO of stub. “Having spent most of my career in financial services focused on small and micro businesses, I kept seeing the same thing — accounting tools were too complicated, too expensive, and too far removed from how people actually run a business day to day,” she told Disrupt Africa. “Traditional accounting platforms are generally built for accountants and established SMEs. They are powerful, but often too complex, time-consuming and costly for a tuck shop, a salon, freelancer or a side hustle.” The real “competition” for most of stub’s market isn’t another platform at all, Dandridge said. “It’s a spreadsheet, a WhatsApp chat, or a shoebox full of slips. That is the gap we spotted at the bottom of the pyramid: millions of micro-entrepreneurs and small business owners who were completely unserved by traditional accounting software,” she said. The startup, which bootstrapped a large portion of its journey until it found strategic angel investors, has secured a few major partnerships, which Dandridge says matter most. “iKhokha was our first big partnership – stub powers their iK Accounting product, and that single anchor relationship has driven a significant number of businesses onto our platform,” she said. “The second was our direct integration with Capitec, South Africa’s largest bank by customer numbers, which puts simple accounting in the hands of a huge base of entrepreneurs and side-hustlers. And most recently, our direct app integration with Yoco, the payment platform for independent business owners across the country.” Going from a single anchor partner, to a direct bank integration, to now working with Yoco in roughly 18 months are milestones Dandridge said she is proud of. “But the most exciting part is the access this creates for entrepreneurs who have never had this level of tooling to run their businesses,” she said. As a result, access has been strong and accelerating. “We’re now supporting just under 10,000 businesses on the stub platform, up from under 4,000 entrepreneurs roughly seven months ago. Signups have compounded every quarter – from a few hundred in 2023 to over 1,800 in a single quarter this year,” said Dandridge. stub’s strongest concentrations are Cape Town, Johannesburg, Pretoria and Durban, and its customer base skews toward micro and informal businesses like tuck shops, spazas, taverns, fast food outlets, salons, cafés, independent consultants and service-based entrepreneurs. “Our name is a statement of intent: stub is built for entrepreneurs. Platforms make global expansion a simpler task, and the playbook stays the same — simple tools distributed through partners and our own direct-to-consumer, on-the-ground channels,” Dandridge said. The startup runs off a subscription model. Businesses use the core tools for free and upgrade to paid plans for the fuller feature set, with payments revenue layered on through payment links. Dandridge said stub has had to overcome plenty of challenges, but has learnt that difficulties usually carry an opportunity inside them. “An unhappy customer is often one of the best teachers; almost every time, they are pointing at something that makes the product better for everyone else. And there’s also a handful of hard “no”s along the way – and more often than not, that pushes us toward something exciting just around the corner,” she said. “So, we try not to treat the hard moments as setbacks. They’ve shaped the product, the team, and how we show up for entrepreneurs – who, after all, deal with this kind of stuff every single day.” Startups: stub Investisseurs: Fonds: Pays: Africa Catégorie: startup

title: LemFi Acquires Wealth8 to Expand into Wealth Management and Investment Services
summary: LemFi, a cross-border financial platform, has received UK FCA approval to acquire investment platform Wealth8, enabling it to offer wealth-building and investment services to its 2 million+ customers. The acquisition marks LemFi's entry into wealthtech and its evolution into a full financial-life platform for diaspora communities.
content: LemFi, the financial platform for people living and working across borders, has secured approval from the UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) for its acquisition of investment platform Wealth8. The approval paves the way for LemFi to add wealth-building and investment to its product ecosystem, marking the company's entry into wealthtech and a significant step in its evolution into a full financial-life platform. LemFi is the trusted platform helping people move money across borders, access credit, and save money across the UK, Europe, North America and Australia. The Wealth8 acquisition adds a missing piece: the ability to grow money over the long term. It continues an arc familiar to millions of people: build life in a new country, support family back home, save, establish a credit footprint, and ultimately invest in a more secure future. With Wealth8, LemFi can now support that journey on a single platform. That momentum is already underway. In 2025, LemFi launched its Instant Access Savings Account, powered by ClearBank: a high-yield product that lets customers earn daily interest, paid monthly, on balances they can access at any time. With promotional rates reaching 5.00% AER, it was a deliberate move to shift from just enabling customers to move money to helping customers keep and grow it. But Savings is only the first step in building lasting wealth; investing is also key, and it is precisely this crucial part of the financial ladder that newcomers are most excluded from. The opportunity is significant. While investing remains one of the UK's most powerful drivers of long-term wealth, participation remains uneven. Most UK adults with more than £10,000 in investible assets (61%) hold at least three-quarters of it in cash rather than investments. The barriers are sharpest for communities that have arrived from abroad. Research from the London School of Economics published in late 2025 found that the UK's ethnic wealth gap has widened over the past decade. Runnymede Trust research revealed that for every £1 of wealth, several minority communities hold as little as 10-20p. The gap reflects unequal income, but also access to the assets that compound into wealth over time. Wealth8 was founded to address exactly this divide, with a mission to make investing simple, affordable, and accessible to communities that mainstream platforms have overlooked, offering minimums as low as £8 and diversified portfolios. Under LemFi, that mission will broaden to reach the wider community of people who live and work across borders, combining Wealth8's investment expertise with LemFi's scale, technology and 2 million+ customer base. The FCA's approval reflects LemFi's growing regulatory footprint and governance capabilities, building on existing authorisations in the UK and approvals across North America, Europe, Australia and key remittance corridors in Africa and Asia. Ridwan Olalere, co-founder and CEO of LemFi, said: "We started LemFi by helping people send money because that was the most urgent need. But financial progress doesn't stop at the transfer. This approval allows us to help customers save, access credit and now invest, supporting them as they build long-term financial security wherever they call home." This acquisition is the latest milestone in LemFi's expansion from a remittance specialist into a multi-product financial platform, following its move into credit, connectivity and savings, as well as a series of market and regulatory approvals over the past year. The company has committed to deepening its presence in the UK, which it sees as a leading global hub for cross-border finance. About LemFi Founded in 2021, LemFi is building the future of financial services for the underserved worldwide. Initially focused on remittances, with over two million customers across Europe, Australia, and North America sending funds to more than 30 emerging markets, the company is expanding into a full-service financial hub for the communities it serves, spanning payments, credit, connectivity, savings, and now investing. LemFi's team consists of over 350 people across four continents, with more than $85 million in funding from investors including Highland Europe.

title: RoboCare: Tunisian startup advancing precision agriculture in Africa
summary: RoboCare, a Tunisian startup, is developing precision agriculture solutions for the African market.
content: RoboCare, a Tunisian startup, is positioning itself in the precision agriculture sector to serve African agriculture. The startup leverages technology to optimize farming practices across the continent.

title: Build Engaging Recruitment Campaigns for Manufacturing Companies Using AI Avatar Employee Ambassadors
summary: A sponsored post on Disrupt Africa discussing how manufacturing companies can use AI avatar employee ambassadors via Pippit to create engaging recruitment campaigns, reduce video production costs, and attract talent through authentic digital storytelling.
content: In the last decade, the method of recruiting potential candidates by manufacturers has drastically changed. It is not just a consideration for job functions and benefits but also for workplace culture, opportunities for advancement, “team environment,” and the daily experience of working in a specific position. In parallel, companies find it difficult to produce recruitment content due to high costs and lengthy processes to schedule employee video interviews, video production and produce well-designed recruitment materials. AI avatar is making an impact because companies are no longer limited to just standard recruitment videos. Now, companies can use digital assistants to deliver authentic and personal messages about their corporate culture quickly while keeping costs down. With Pippit, companies can turn their job openings into high-quality videos that appear polished and authentic. This will allow companies to hire not only machine operators, engineers, technicians, and new hires but to also portray a consistent company story across all recruitment efforts. When your best recruiter never needs a camera crew Employees sharing their work experience and happy thoughts via testimonials are generally the most successful way to recruit talent. Prospective candidates are interested in hearing real-life experiences, specifically regarding career development opportunities and the environment created within the organization. However, filming traditional testimonial videos can be a challenge due to scheduling conflicts between busy workloads and production timelines as well as constantly needing new content to promote recruiting efforts. Digital Employee Ambassadors allow businesses to create polished recruiting videos without needing to schedule more shoot days with employees. Rather than schedule additional video shoots, each company’s written script can be modified accordingly to provide an up-to-date and relevant recruiting video based on the company’s current recruitment needs. Giving every department a voice In big manufacturing plants, various departments do vastly different work. From production teams and quality controllers to techs, logisticians, and engineers, everyone brings a distinct view to the table. These digital figureheads help show off each division’s vibe and duties. At the same time, it keeps everything looking cohesive with the broader employer image across all hiring stuff. Bringing factory careers into the spotlight Many job hunters still think factories mean boring assembly lines and no room to grow. Yet, today’s plants use automation, robots, and fancy tech, making roles super different from what people traditionally assume. Firms fight these old ideas through recruitment vids that show teamwork and ways to build a career. When prospects learn about real modern setups, they’re way more interested in jobs they might’ve skipped before. It helps to paint a bigger picture than just listing duties. Storytelling does wonders for attracting new hires because most want to hear about future opportunities. For instance, sharing tales of workers rising from basics gigs to top spots makes a huge impact. This lets potential new faces dream of where they could end up in the company too. Making recruitment consistent across every hiring campaign Larger manufacturing firms commonly look to hire for numerous roles in various places. Keeping messages uniform gets tricky since diverse recruiters and teams make their own content. Digital brand ambassadors help by providing all candidates with consistent info about firm principles, work norms, perks, and job openings. This boosts brand image while cutting down on confusion during the application process. Plus, pre-interview videos address uncertainties early on. Prospective hires often question workplace climates, team dynamics, and growth prospects. By answering these concerns ahead of time, candidates can feel better prepared and at ease before their interviews even start. Connecting with the next generation of manufacturing talent Younger job hunters now mostly check out info on video-first sites. Traditional hiring stuff might not hook them like snazzy visual content would. Videos in campaigns go with what young folks prefer these days. This lets factories reach recruits in relatable ways. It really helps for luring in students, apprentices, tech trainees, and young pros. Plus, using video makes those tech jobs clear.

When your best recruiter never needs a camera crew Employees sharing their work experience and happy thoughts via testimonials are generally the most successful way to recruit talent. Prospective candidates are interested in hearing real-life experiences, specifically regarding career development opportunities and the environment created within the organization. However, filming traditional testimonial videos can be a challenge due to scheduling conflicts between busy workloads and production timelines as well as constantly needing new content to promote recruiting efforts. Rather than schedule additional video shoots, each company’s written script can be modified accordingly to provide an up-to-date and relevant recruiting video based on the company’s current recruitment needs. Giving every department a voice In big manufacturing plants, various departments do vastly different work. From production teams and quality controllers to techs, logisticians, and engineers, everyone brings a distinct view to the table. These digital figureheads help show off each division’s vibe and duties. At the same time, it keeps everything looking cohesive with the broader employer image across all hiring stuff. title: Tunisia’s RoboCare secures funding to accelerate precision agriculture in Africa, Middle East
summary: Tunisian precision agriculture startup RoboCare has raised a six-figure investment from 216 Capital to expand its AI-driven farm management platform across African and Middle Eastern markets.
content: Tunisian startup RoboCare, founded in Sfax, develops an agricultural management platform that leverages satellite imagery, drone data, IoT sensors, weather data, and field expertise to help farmers detect crop diseases early, optimize resource usage, and improve farm performance. The company has secured a six-figure funding round from 216 Capital to support its next growth phase and expansion into African and Middle Eastern markets. Hassen Arfaoui, principal at 216 Capital, stated the investment aligns with the fund's strategy of backing high-potential tech startups addressing the continent's major economic, social, and environmental challenges.

title: Tajani pour une «alliance stratégique» entre l'Italie et la Tunisie
summary: Le ministre italien des Affaires étrangères Antonio Tajani a appelé à une alliance stratégique entre l'Italie et la Tunisie lors du Forum d'affaires Tunisie-Italie à Tunis. Il a encouragé les entrepreneurs italiens à investir en Tunisie et vice versa, soulignant que les échanges commerciaux ont atteint 6,5 milliards d'euros en 2025 et que les entreprises italiennes emploient 85 000 Tunisiens. Tajani a présenté cela comme une «diplomatie de la croissance» favorisant les coentreprises et l'attraction des investissements.
content: Le ministre italien des Affaires étrangères, Antonio Tajani, a déclaré lors du Forum d'affaires Tunisie-Italie qui s'est ouvert le mercredi 25 juin 2026 à Tunis : «J'encourage les entrepreneurs italiens à faire confiance à la Tunisie, à y investir et à s'intéresser de près au pays ; le gouvernement mettra tout en œuvre pour faciliter cette démarche», tout comme «nous aiderons les entrepreneurs tunisiens souhaitant investir en Italie à surmonter les obstacles afin que cette collaboration puisse prospérer». Il a affirmé que l'Italie et la Tunisie sont «deux rives d'une même mer» qu'il faut rapprocher «au nom de la croissance, de la compétitivité et de la prospérité». Tajani a souligné que le volume des échanges commerciaux a atteint 6,5 milliards d'euros en 2025 et continue de croître en 2026. Il a mentionné que les entreprises italiennes emploient 85 000 collaborateurs tunisiens et que la Tunisie est devenue un partenaire de plus en plus important pour une croissance partagée. Il a évoqué une «diplomatie de la croissance» favorisant les coentreprises, les exportations et l'attraction des investissements, et a annoncé la publication d'un ouvrage consacré à la Tunisie comme destination pour les intérêts économiques italiens.

title: Les raisons de l'échec des entreprises communautaires en Tunisie
summary: Analyse des causes structurelles de l'échec des entreprises communautaires en Tunisie : capital insuffisant, agenda politique, réticence bancaire, dépendance au pouvoir central et manque d'expérience des fondateurs.
content: Les entreprises communautaires sont un échec. Je l'expliquais déjà il y a des années et je le répète aujourd'hui… L'avantage même que leur accorde le décret présidant à leur création est l'une des causes de leur échec. Lorsque nous désignons ce type d'entreprise et le présentons comme une solution alternative pour la création de richesse et le développement local, au détriment du tissu économique constitué par d'autres types d'entreprises, nous condamnons non seulement un système économique existant à l'échec, mais aussi un nouveau système mort-né. Sami Jallouli * Au-delà de l'aspect législatif et réglementaires, analysons les raisons structurelles de cet échec : 1. La plupart de ces entreprises disposent de ressources d'autofinancement très limitées. Il est illogique de créer une société avec 50 associés et un capital qui n'excède parfois pas 20 000 dinars. Cela traduit une volonté de mutualiser les ressources sans modèle économique clair garantissant la pérennité de l'activité. Même en cas de succès, la répartition des bénéfices entre un si grand nombre d'associés rendrait les rendements individuels négligeables, décourageant ainsi les actionnaires de développer l'entreprise et affaiblissant leur sens des responsabilités et leur propension à prendre des risques. Ceci conduit souvent à une négligence de la gestion ou à un abandon au premier obstacle… 2. L'une des principales raisons de l'échec de ce modèle réside dans son lien avec un agenda politique plutôt que commercial ou d'investissement. Historiquement, la Tunisie a une expérience douloureuse avec ce type d'organisation économique. Le mouvement coopératif (promu dans les années 1960 par Ahmed Ben Slah, Ndlr) et les tragédies qu'il a engendrées restent une plaie ouverte dans la conscience collective, plus de 50 ans après son échec. La problématique des entreprises communautaires en Tunisie 3. Les banques abordent ces entreprises avec une extrême prudence, les considérant comme des projets politiques dépourvus de viabilité commerciale. La plupart des banques appartenant au secteur privé, elles rechignent à risquer les fonds de leurs partenaires sur un avenir incertain. En s'abstenant de financer ces entreprises, les banques privées les rendent dépendantes du soutien public, ce qui s'apparente à une recherche de rente et perpétue leur soumission au pouvoir central. 4. Bien que ces sociétés soient régies par un décret présidentiel, l'idée même que leur émergence soit liée à une décision politique imposée d'en haut constitue une faille majeure. L'expérience tunisienne avec des mécanismes de financement similaires, tels que la Banque de solidarité et le Fonds 26-26, en est un exemple frappant. Malgré leur dynamisme relatif, un pourcentage significatif des projets qu'ils ont financés a échoué, comme en témoigne le volume des prêts n'ayant pas été remboursés. Parce que ces sociétés privées sont liées à la vision du président, elles sont devenues les victimes d'un conflit entre ceux qui les soutiennent par simple loyauté et ceux qui les entravent pour des raisons politiques… ce qui engendre un climat d'investissement instable. 5. La plupart des fondateurs sont des jeunes ou des personnes sans emploi qui manquent d'expérience en matière de gestion de grandes entreprises, de logistique et de marketing. Ce manque d'expérience constitue un obstacle majeur pour toute entreprise naissante. Nommer des personnes sans emploi à la tête d'entreprises par

title: Session de contrôle du Bac | Trois jours de cours de soutien sur la plateforme «Joussour»
summary: Le ministère de l'Éducation tunisien lance un dispositif d'accompagnement pour les élèves ajournés au baccalauréat via la plateforme éducative Joussour, avec des cours intensifs du 26 au 28 juin 2026 en préparation de la session de contrôle.
content: Un dispositif d'accompagnement et de soutien scolaire au profit des élèves ajournés de la session principale du baccalauréat est désormais en place sur la plateforme éducative « Joussour ». C'est ce qu'a annoncé le ministère de l'Éducation ce jeudi, en précisant que les séances seront ouvertes du 26 au 28 juin en préparation des examens prévus les 29 et 30 juin et les 1er et 2 juillet 2026. L'initiative, qui couvre l'ensemble des filières, propose une série de cours intensifs, de révisions ciblées et de séances d'orientation animés par des cadres éducatifs et pédagogiques pour aider les candidats à aborder la session de contrôle dans les meilleures conditions.

C'est ce qu'a annoncé le ministère de l'Éducation ce jeudi, en précisant que les séances seront ouvertes du 26 au 28 juin en préparation des examens prévus les 29 et 30 juin et les 1er et 2 juillet 2026. L'initiative, qui couvre l'ensemble des filières, propose une série de cours intensifs, de révisions ciblées et de séances d'orientation animés par des cadres éducatifs et pédagogiques pour aider les candidats à aborder la session de contrôle dans les meilleures conditions. title: Kenyan startups Fuzu, Kyosk secure funding from Jobtech Alliance
summary: Kenyan startups Fuzu and Kyosk have secured undisclosed funding from the Jobtech Alliance, an ecosystem-building initiative steered by Mercy Corps and BFA Global. Fuzu, a talent infrastructure platform pivoting to managed AI data operations, and Kyosk, a B2B commerce platform serving over 200,000 informal retailers across East Africa, are the latest additions to the alliance's portfolio.
content: Kenyan startups Fuzu and Kyosk are the latest to secure an undisclosed amount of funding from the Jobtech Alliance. Disrupt Africa reported in October 2022 on the launch of the Jobtech Alliance, an ecosystem-building initiative around inclusive jobtech in Africa steered by Mercy Corps and BFA Global, which aims to create an enabling environment for entrepreneurs to build platforms that deliver quality livelihoods, are inclusive, and enable users to engage in decent work. It has run several cohorts, but is now also making direct investments in relevant ventures, most recently Nigeria's Bumpa and Kenya's Flowcart, both e-commerce ventures, and Kenyan social commerce startup Twiva. It has now made two further investments, in Fuzu and Kyosk. Fuzu has spent more than a decade building talent infrastructure across Africa, and has now pivoted from running a marketplace to managing global teams for AI data operations, evaluation, and QA work. The investment came out of the Jobtech Alliance's Digital Work Sector Scan, which mapped how AI is reshaping African digital work. Over the next six months, the alliance will work with Fuzu on direct international client growth, refining the positioning around Fuzu Atlas, and moving up the value chain into higher-quality AI-adjacent work. "Most African talent platforms still operate as marketplaces, competing on volume and cheaper sourcing. Fuzu has already moved past that. They've built the infrastructure layer underneath the marketplace, and Fuzu Atlas is now using it to deliver managed teams that global clients can actually build with. It's one of the more credible answers we've seen to where good digital jobs in Africa come from next," said Nick Markham, investment lead at the Jobtech Alliance. The second new portfolio company is Kyosk, a B2B commerce platform that serves over 200,000 informal retailers across Kenya, Nigeria, Uganda, and Tanzania. Kyosk sits inside the alliance's microenterprise investment thesis – platforms that operate inside the daily workflow challenges of informal retailers, from inventory access to delivery consistency to repeat purchasing behaviour. "African B2B commerce is maturing, and the platforms that win from here will be the ones that learn to bridge the fragmented workflows of informal retailers: inventory access, delivery reliability, repeat ordering. Kyosk's shift from 25 warehouses in Kenya to one per country, with a sharper focus on route-level economics, is exactly the kind of operational discipline this market needs," said Mide Alonge, senior venture builder at the Jobtech Alliance.

title: Projet en faveur de l'emploi des « Jeunes binationaux » tuniso-italiens
summary: L'UTICA et le Delta Center ont signé un protocole d'accord pour le projet « Giovani Binazionali » visant à faciliter l'intégration professionnelle des jeunes citoyens italo-tunisiens en Tunisie et en Italie, lors du Forum économique Tunisie-Italie du 25 juin 2025.
content: En marge du Forum économique et d'affaires Tunisie-Italie, qui s'est tenu à Tunis ce jeudi 25 juin 2025, le président de l'Union tunisienne de l'industrie, du commerce et de l'artisanat (Utica), Samir Majoul, et Sandro Fratini, fondateur et président du Delta Center, un centre d'affaires consacré à la promotion des investissements et des relations économiques entre l'Italie, l'Europe et l'Afrique, ont signé un protocole d'accord visant à collaborer sur le projet « Giovani Binazionali » (« Jeunes binationaux »). La cérémonie de signature s'est déroulée en présence de la cheffe du gouvernement Sarra Zaafrani Zenzeri et du ministre italien des Affaires étrangères et de la Coopération internationale, Antonio Tajani. Cette initiative, destinée à faciliter l'intégration des jeunes citoyens italo-tunisiens résidant en Tunisie dans le secteur industriel, marque une nouvelle étape dans le renforcement des relations entre l'Italie et la Tunisie. La phase finale du projet se déroulera en juillet, à l'occasion d'un événement organisé par le Comites Tunisia (Com.It.Es) à Tunis. Le projet — mis en œuvre par le Com.It.Es Tunisie avec le soutien de la Direction générale pour les services aux citoyens à l'étranger et les politiques migratoires (DGIT) du ministère italien des Affaires étrangères et de la Coopération internationale — vise à réaliser une enquête structurée sur les compétences professionnelles et les parcours éducatifs des jeunes citoyens italo-tunisiens vivant en Tunisie. L'objectif est de valoriser leur potentiel et de faciliter leur insertion sur le marché du travail, tant en Tunisie qu'en Italie. Grâce à une plateforme numérique et à des actions de contact direct, une base de données sera constituée au service des institutions, des entreprises et des organismes de formation, générant ainsi des retombées concrètes en matière d'employabilité et de coopération économique entre les deux pays. « C'est un projet sur lequel nous travaillons depuis décembre dernier et qui entre désormais dans sa phase finale », a déclaré M. Fratini, précisant que le Comites a recueilli « des dizaines et des dizaines de CV » de jeunes binationaux en Tunisie. Selon M. Fratini, le projet a également permis d'entrer en contact avec des personnes ayant récemment redécouvert leurs liens juridiques avec l'Italie. « Beaucoup d'entre eux, entre autres, ont récemment découvert qu'ils possédaient la citoyenneté italienne et détenaient donc un passeport italien », a déclaré le président du Comites. Après les étapes de collecte et de sélection, le projet va maintenant passer à une phase plus opérationnelle, grâce à la mise en réseau avec le tissu économique tunisien et italo-tunisien. « À la suite de ce recensement, nous avons sélectionné une cinquantaine de binationaux et conclu un accord avec l'Utica », a expliqué M. Fratini à l'Agenzia Nova, ajoutant qu' « un second accord sera ensuite signé avec la Chambre de commerce tuniso-italienne ». Lors d'un événement prévu en juillet, le Comites remettra les CV sélectionnés aux deux organisations professionnelles. « Ces organisations transmettront les CV à leurs membres — entrepreneurs italiens, tunisiens et mixtes — afin de les aider à trouver un emploi en Tunisie », a conclu M. Fratini.

title: Anava commits $5.2M to Silicon Badia's second regional venture capital fund BIF2
summary: Anava, the fund of funds managed by Smart Capital under the Startup Tunisia program, commits $5.2 million to Badia Impact Squared Fund (BIF2) managed by Silicon Badia. BIF2 is a sector-agnostic fund investing in Series A and Series B technology startups across Egypt, Jordan, Tunisia, Morocco, and Lebanon.
content: Smart Capital, the management company of Anava fund of funds, announces a $5.2M commitment to venture capital fund Badia Impact Squared Fund (BIF2) managed by Silicon Badia, a pioneer venture capital group investing in early and growth-stage technology companies operating globally. Anava has a target size of €100M (with a first closing of 40 million euros fully subscribed by Caisse des Dépôts et Consignations (CDC)) and aims to invest in over 13 VC funds dedicated to startups at their seed, early and late stages of development. Anava is one of the three pillars of a national program, Startup Tunisia, namely Startup Act (the incentives), Startup Ecosystem (the supporting structures) and Startup Invest (the funding including Anava) whose management is entrusted to Smart Capital. With offices in the US and the Middle East region, Silicon Badia has invested in over 80 technology startups in more than 15 different industries and cities worldwide. Their second regional Fund BIF2 is a sector-agnostic fund that invests in technology and tech-enabled startups in Series A and Series B stages in Egypt, Jordan, Tunisia, Morocco, and Lebanon.

title: BioNTech to Acquire InstaDeep to Strengthen Pioneering Position in the Field of AI-powered Drug Discovery, Design and Development
summary: BioNTech announced an agreement to acquire InstaDeep, a global AI and machine learning technology company, for approximately £362 million upfront plus up to £200 million in performance-based milestones. The acquisition aims to enhance BioNTech's AI-driven drug discovery capabilities and add around 240 skilled professionals to its workforce.
content: MAINZ, Germany, and LONDON, United Kingdom, January 09, 2023 – BioNTech SE (Nasdaq: BNTX, “BioNTech”) and InstaDeep Ltd. (“InstaDeep”) today announced that they have entered into an agreement under which BioNTech will acquire InstaDeep, a leading global technology company in the field of artificial intelligence (“AI”) and machine learning (“ML”). The transaction includes a total upfront consideration of approximately £362 million in cash and BioNTech shares to acquire 100% of the remaining InstaDeep shares, excluding the shares already owned by BioNTech. In addition, InstaDeep shareholders will be eligible to receive additional performance-based future milestone payments up to approximately £200 million. The transaction follows BioNTech’s initial equity investment as part of InstaDeep’s Series B financing round in January 2022. The acquisition supports BioNTech’s strategy to build world-leading capabilities in AI-driven drug discovery and development of next-generation immunotherapies and vaccines to address diseases with high unmet medical need. The transaction will combine two organizations with a common culture and is expected to add approximately 240 highly skilled professionals to BioNTech’s workforce, including teams in AI, ML, bioengineering, data science, and software development. Through the acquisition, BioNTech will grow its network of global research collaborators in the field and expand its footprint in key talent hubs across the United States, Europe, Africa and the Middle East. With the acquisition of InstaDeep, validated and novel BioNTech-trained AI- and ML-based models are planned to be embedded across BioNTech’s discovery platforms and connected, through InstaDeep’s DeepChain™ platform, to an integrated automated lab infrastructure. This has the objective to enable high-throughput design and testing of novel drug candidates at scale. In addition, BioNTech plans to develop novel AI and ML technology solutions which it aims to apply across key strategic and operational functions.
document: Titre: BioNTech to Acquire InstaDeep to Strengthen Pioneering Position in the Field of AI-powered Drug Discovery, Design and Development Résumé: BioNTech announced an agreement to acquire InstaDeep, a global AI and machine learning technology company, for approximately £362 million upfront plus up to £200 million in performance-based milestones. The acquisition aims to enhance BioNTech's AI-driven drug discovery capabilities and add around 240 skilled professionals to its workforce. Contenu: MAINZ, Germany, and LONDON, United Kingdom, January 09, 2023 – BioNTech SE (Nasdaq: BNTX, “BioNTech”) and InstaDeep Ltd. (“InstaDeep”) today announced that they have entered into an agreement under which BioNTech will acquire InstaDeep, a leading global technology company in the field of artificial intelligence (“AI”) and machine learning (“ML”). The transaction includes a total upfront consideration of approximately £362 million in cash and BioNTech shares to acquire 100% of the remaining InstaDeep shares, excluding the shares already owned by BioNTech. In addition, InstaDeep shareholders will be eligible to receive additional performance-based future milestone payments up to approximately £200 million. The transaction follows BioNTech’s initial equity investment as part of InstaDeep’s Series B financing round in January 2022. The acquisition supports BioNTech’s strategy to build world-leading capabilities in AI-driven drug discovery and development of next-generation immunotherapies and vaccines to address diseases with high unmet medical need. The transaction will combine two organizations with a common culture and is expected to add approximately 240 highly skilled professionals to BioNTech’s workforce, including teams in AI, ML, bioengineering, data science, and software development. Through the acquisition, BioNTech will grow its network of global research collaborators in the field and expand its footprint in key talent hubs across the United States, Europe, Africa and the Middle East. With the acquisition of InstaDeep, validated and novel BioNTech-trained AI- and ML-based models are planned to be embedded across BioNTech’s discovery platforms and connected, through InstaDeep’s DeepChain™ platform, to an integrated automated lab infrastructure. This has the objective to enable high-throughput design and testing of novel drug candidates at scale. In addition, BioNTech plans to develop novel AI and ML technology solutions which it aims to apply across key strategic and operational functions. Startups: InstaDeep Investisseurs: BioNTech Fonds: Pays: Tunisia Catégorie: funding

title: 2nd session of AIR and AIR² : 17 projects selected
summary: Startup Tunisia's Flywheel program, operated with partners CDC, GIZ, and the World Bank, announced 17 grants in its second 2021 session: 11 early-stage startups received AIR proof-of-concept grants, and 6 seed-stage startups received AIR² investment readiness grants.
content: For its second session of the year, the Flywheel program operated by Startup Tunisia and its partners, the CDC, GIZ and the World Bank has just announced the awarding of 17 grants divided between AIR and AIR². Indeed, 11 solutions have been selected to receive AIR, the grant supporting the Proof-Of-Concept development of very early stage Startups. AIR compensates the lack of financing opportunities and the scarcity of business angels funding at the earliest phase of their lifecycles. The 11 projects awarded AIR grants are: CO-SOLAR, YAGRI, ASQII, Pepolls, Design and sale of remote energy management systems, Vife, BLASTER, OZONE SOLUTIONS, Fouita, iCan-C, Kids Protect, Smart House Tunisia. In addition, 6 startups, more advanced in their development cycle, have been selected to benefit from AIR². AIR² is an investment readiness grant awarded to seed stage startups aiming to reach and raise a Series A round. The 6 startups selected are: Dabchy, Wattnow, Datavora, Digiconstat, Ezzayra, Wantotrip. AIR and AIR² are awarded following a call for application process and evaluation by an independent and tech-savvy selection committee. Next session will open on January 3rd, 2022.

startups: Design and sale of remote energy management systems

title: Participation of Tunisian companies and startups in the "Mobile World Congress" exhibition
summary: Tunisia participates for the 8th consecutive year in the Mobile World Congress (MWC), the largest mobile industry event, held June 28–July 1, 2021 in Barcelona. The Tunisian pavilion hosts 5 companies and 2 labeled startups selected via a call for applications organized with the Startup Tunisia program.
content: Tunisia participates for the 8th consecutive year in the exhibition "Mobile World Congress", the largest meeting of the cell phone industry and telecoms, held from June 28 to July 1, 2021 in Barcelona, said Monday in Tunis, the Center for Export Promotion (CEPEX). Thus, the Tunisian stand houses 5 companies and 2 startups labeled selected, following a call for applications launched in collaboration with the program Startup Tunisia.

title: Official launch of the "ANAVA" Fund of Funds
summary: Smart Capital, the World Bank, CDC, and GIZ officially launched the ANAVA Fund of Funds, a $75 million (207.19 million dinars) project funded by the World Bank over seven years to support the Startup Tunisia program and catalyze innovative startups, economic growth, and youth employment.
content: Smart Capital, the WB, the CDC, and the GIZ officially launched the project "Start-up and Innovative SMEs" and the ANAVA fund of funds. The launch took place during a press conference at the Baron d'Erlanger Palace in Sidi Bou Said. The project is funded by the World Bank to the tune of 75 million dollars. Or 207.19 million dinars. It will be implemented over seven years. It was designed to support the government program "Startup Tunisia" which aims to catalyze the creation and growth of innovative startups. And this, while stimulating economic and employment opportunities for Tunisian youth.

title: InstaDeep raises $100 million from the world's largest investors
summary: Tunisian AI startup InstaDeep closed a $100 million Series B funding round led by Alpha Intelligence Capital and CDIB, with participation from BioNTech, Chimera Abu Dhabi, Deutsche Bahn Digital Ventures, Google, G42, and Synergy.
content: Tunisian startup InstaDeep announced on January 25, 2022, that it has raised $100 million in funding and closed a Series B round that was led by DeepTech investment firm Alpha Intelligence Capital, accompanied by CDIB. Investors in this round include a number of the world's largest investors, namely BioNTech, Chimera Abu Dhabi, Deutsche Bahn Digital Ventures, Google, G42 and Synergy. "We are very pleased with the caliber of investors. We want to show them that we are truly trustworthy and that we can work together to change the world," Zohra Slim, co-founder of InstaDeep, told us.

title: FinTech app built to help students manage finances raises $40M Series B
summary: San Francisco-based fintech startup Mos, founded by Tunisian entrepreneur Amira Yahyaoui, raised a $40 million Series B round led by Tiger Global, valuing the company at $400 million. Other investors include Sequoia, Khaled Helioui, Expa, Emerson Collective, and Lux Capital.
content: San Francisco-based Mos just announced a $40 million Series B to grow its platform that provides financial services to students. The round valued the startup at $400 million and was led by Tiger Global. Other investors included Sequoia, Khaled Helioui, Expa, Emerson Collective and Lux Capital, according to the announcement. It was previously valued at $48 million after its Series A in early 2020, according to PitchBook. Mos was founded in 2017 by Amira Yahyaoui, 37, who named the company after the fictional town of Mos Espa from Star Wars, according to its website. She's also from Tataouine, Tunisia — the real life inspiration for Luke Skywalker's fictional home.

title: Wattnow raises $1.3 million in pre-series A funding
summary: Tunisian startup Wattnow closed a $1.3 million pre-Series A round led by Katapult, 216 Capital Ventures, Launch Africa Ventures, and a follow-on from Oman Technology Fund. CrossBoundary and an angel investor also participated. The round was preceded by a bridge from Bridging Angels and support from Startup Tunisia via the Air2 program.
content: We are pleased to announce that Wattnow has just closed a $1.3 million pre-series A round led by Katapult, 216 Capital Ventures, Launch Africa Ventures and a follow-on from Oman Technology Fund. CrossBoundary and an angel investor also participated. This round was preceded by a very important bridge from Bridging Angels and the support of Startup Tunisia in the form of the Air2 program.

title: How Akuna Wallet, Trenderz want to fix creator payments in Africa
summary: Africa's creator economy faces significant payment infrastructure gaps, excluding millions of Francophone African creators from platform monetization. Startups like Akuna Wallet, Trenderz, and Mainstack are building payment infrastructure to enable cross-border payouts, standardized contracts, and reliable payment systems for creators in countries like Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, and DRC.
content: Africa's creator economy has a payment problem. These startups want to fix it. By Lina Kacyem, 2nd Jun, 2026. Africa hosts millions of digital content creators, a booming social media landscape, and one of the planet's youngest populations. Yet financial infrastructure to pay them fairly is virtually non-existent. The creator economy on the continent is expanding rapidly. According to the International School of Advertising, the creator economy market was valued between $3 and $5.1 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach $29.84 billion by 2032, with a CAGR of 28.7%. Smartphones are proliferating rapidly. TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube audiences are growing faster in Africa than anywhere else. Yet for the vast majority of African creators — particularly those in Francophone West Africa — one question remains unanswered: how to get paid? The most visible expression of this gap is the exclusion of most African countries from direct monetization programs on platforms where creators build their livelihoods. In 2026, the YouTube Partner Program is available in over 120 countries, but eligibility is not universal — creators must reside in a supported region to access monetization. For creators in Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, or the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) — countries with tens of millions of social media users — YouTube ad revenue remains structurally inaccessible through conventional channels. Reasons accumulate: global platforms offering monetization often require an international bank account or do not accept local banks for payouts. PayPal coverage is partial or non-existent. Stripe remains unavailable in most Francophone African markets. A creator with 500,000 subscribers in Abidjan, building audiences for international brands, still cannot receive a YouTube payout directly to a local bank account. Although Africa represents 18% of the world's population, the continent captures only about 5.4% of global content creation revenue — a gap explained not by production volume or audience engagement, but by payment infrastructure failures. The problem extends beyond platform payouts. Influencer marketing in the region operates through a fragmented network of WhatsApp negotiations, cash payments, in-kind arrangements — dinners and hotel stays as fees — and informal one-off transfers that can take weeks to execute. There are no standardized contracts, no performance tracking infrastructure, no reliable cross-border payment system. The result: an economically significant market still operating artisanally. Building the pipes: Akuna, Mainstack and the new infrastructure players. A new generation of platforms is attempting to build this missing infrastructure, attacking the problem from different angles.

Yet financial infrastructure to pay them fairly is virtually non-existent. The creator economy on the continent is expanding rapidly. According to the International School of Advertising, the creator economy market was valued between $3 and $5.1 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach $29.84 billion by 2032, with a CAGR of 28.7%. Smartphones are proliferating rapidly. TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube audiences are growing faster in Africa than anywhere else. Yet for th

ected to reach $29.84 billion by 2032, with a CAGR of 28.7%. Smartphones are proliferating rapidly. TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube audiences are growing faster in Africa than anywhere else. Yet for the vast majority of African creators — particularly those in Francophone West Africa — one question remains unanswered: how to get paid? The most visible expression of this gap is the exclusion of most African countries from direct monetization programs on platforms where creators build their livelihoods. In 2026, the YouTube Partner Program is available in over 120 countries, but eligibility is not universal — creators must reside in a supported region to access monetization. For creators in Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, or the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) — countries with tens of millions of social media users — YouTube ad revenue remains structurally inaccessible through conventional channels. Reasons accumulate: global platforms offering monetization often require an international bank account or do not accept local banks for payouts. PayPal coverage is partial or non-existent. Stripe remains unavailable in most Francophone African markets. A creator with 500,000 subscribers

al bank account or do not accept local banks for payouts. PayPal coverage is partial or non-existent. Stripe remains unavailable in most Francophone African markets. A creator with 500,000 subscribers in Abidjan, building audiences for international brands, still cannot receive a YouTube payout directly to a local bank account. Although Africa represents 18% of the world's population, the continent captures only about 5.4% of global content creation revenue — a gap explained not by production volume or audience engagement, but by payment infrastructure failures. The problem extends beyond platform payouts. Influencer marketing in the region operates through a fragmented network of WhatsApp negotiations, cash payments, in-kind arrangements — dinners and hotel stays as fees — and informal one-off transfers that can take weeks to execute. There are no standardized contracts, no performance tracking infrastructure, no reliable cross-border payment system. The result: an economically significant market still operating artisanally. Building the pipes: Akuna, Mainstack and the new infrastructure players. A new generation of platforms is attempting to build this missing infrastructure, att

icant market still operating artisanally. Building the pipes: Akuna, Mainstack and the new infrastructure players. A new generation of platforms is attempting to build this missing infrastructure, attacking the problem from different angles. Startups: Akuna Wallet, Trenderz, Mainstack Investisseurs: Fonds: Pays: Africa Catégorie: startup

title: Startup Tunisia: The Fund of Funds' launch event
summary: The official launch of the projects "Startups and Innovative SMEs" and "Flywheel", two funding mechanisms and support for startups, marked a major turning point for Tunisia's innovation ecosystem. The ceremony took place at Ennejma Ezzahra Palace in Sidi Bou Said, attended by the Minister of Technology and Communication Fadhel Kraiem, German Ambassador Peter Prügel, World Bank head of operations Tony Verheijen, Smart Capital Chairman Samir Saied, and CDC Director General Boutheina Ben Yaghlane.
content: Tuesday, March 23 will forever mark a major turning point for the innovation ecosystem in Tunisia. It was the day of the official launch of the projects "Startups and Innovative SMEs" and "Flywheel", two funding mechanisms and support for startups and beating heart of the program Startup Tunisia. The launch ceremony took place at the Ennejma Ezzahra Palace in Sidi Bou Said, in the presence of the Minister of Technology and Communication, Fadhel Kraiem, His Excellency the German Ambassador to Tunisia Peter Prügel, the head of operations for Tunisia at the World Bank, Tony Verheijen, the Chairman of the Board of Directors of Smart Capital, Samir Saied, and the Director General of the Caisse des Dépôts et Consignations (CDC), Boutheina Ben Yaghlane.

title: AVIDEA completes a round of financing of 2 million dinars
summary: Tunisian insurtech startup AVIDEA raised 2 million dinars in a Pre-Series A round led by Capsa Capital Partners and 216 CAPITAL FUND I to scale its digital claims and fraud detection products.
content: The Tunisian startup AVIDEA announced to have closed a Pre-Series A round of financing of 2 million dinars led by one of the main investors in Private Equity in Tunisia, Capsa Capital Partners, and the specialized investment fund 216 CAPITAL FUND I managed by the management company 216 CAPITAL VENTURES. The products currently offered by AVIDEA namely DigiClaim and DigiFraud, respectively for the digitization of the automobile claim and the fight against fraud, being already validated and in production at its customers, the Startup is now moving towards a decisive phase of industrialization and internalization. Created in 2017, AVIDEA is a startup specialized in the digitalization of the automobile claim and the fight against fraud. Its main mission is to help insurance companies reduce the cost of the car claim while offering a better user experience to their customers.

title: Spotify Bets On AI Podcasts, Smarter Ads, And Fan Experiences At 2026 Investor Day
summary: At its 2026 Investor Day, Spotify outlined a strategy to become an all-in-one media and AI powerhouse, centered on its "Large Taste Model" that processes 3.4 trillion daily taste signals. Key announcements included AI-generated personal podcasts, adaptive fitness-guided audio sessions, a "Reserved" feature for early concert ticket access, interactive podcast Q&A for Premium users, paid podcast memberships, expansion of its audiobook catalog to 700,000+ titles, and a rebuilt advertising infrastructure driving 68% year-over-year advertiser growth.
content: If you still think of Spotify as just a place to stream your favorite playlists, think again. At its 2026 Investor Day, the company dropped a massive hint about its future, making it clear they are officially moving past the “music app” label. Instead, Spotify is aiming for something much bigger: an all-in-one media and AI powerhouse fueled by hyper-personalization, creator tools, and your unique taste. At the center of that strategy is what Spotify calls its “Large Taste Model,” an AI system trained on billions of user interactions across music, podcasts, and audiobooks. The company says it now processes roughly 3.4 trillion daily taste signals, data it wants to use to push listeners beyond passive consumption and toward more interactive experiences. The shift is already showing up in product announcements. Spotify introduced AI-generated “Personal Podcasts,” audio programs built around user prompts and listening habits. It also previewed fitness-guided sessions that automatically adapt music tempo and coaching cues to match a workout pace. Another upcoming feature, called “Reserved,” will allow top fans to access concert tickets before public sale through partnerships tied to artist engagement data. Podcasting remains a major part of the company’s expansion plan, though Spotify now wants podcasts to behave more like interactive software than static audio files. Premium users in select markets can already ask questions about podcast episodes in real time while listening. The company is also introducing paid podcast memberships aimed at helping creators build recurring revenue streams directly inside Spotify’s ecosystem. Audiobooks are getting similar treatment. Spotify said its audiobook catalog has grown to more than 700,000 titles across 22 markets, and the company plans to expand audiobook subscription tiers later this year. The less flashy part of the presentation may matter more financially. Spotify spent considerable time discussing its rebuilt advertising infrastructure, which the company says is now driving stronger advertiser growth and improved monetization. Executives said active advertisers rose 68% year over year, while biddable ad channels now account for more than a third of ad revenue. What stood out was how aggressively Spotify framed AI as part of the listening experience itself rather than a background recommendation engine. For years, streaming companies competed on catalog size and distribution. Spotify now appears to think the next phase depends on whether platforms can make media more adaptive, conversational, and personalized. That creates a different kind of competition because Spotify is no longer positioning itself only against music streaming services. It is edging toward territory occupied by AI assistants, creator platforms, live entertainment networks, and digital media companies all at once.

title: Flywheel: the new support program for startups support organizations
summary: Smart Capital launched Flywheel, a 7-year support program for startup support organizations in Tunisia, with two instruments: Deal (grant up to 200k TND for new coaching programs) and Sail (performance-based grants for existing structures).
content: During the official launch ceremony of the Anava fund of funds, Smart Capital unveiled a new support program for startups and support structures. Called Flywheel, this program will be spread over 7 years. Thus, two instruments will be addressed to support structures. The first instrument, called Deal, aims to support the launch of new coaching programs. To do this, it offers a grant worth 200 thousand dinars according to eligibility criteria that will assess the value proposition of this program, the key skills of the team, vision, structuring, cohesion, and connection with other structures and programs in the ecosystem. The second instrument, Sail, will allow existing coaching structures to benefit from a grant whose value is based on performance metrics such as the number of coached startups that have received the label, or the number of coached startups that have raised funds. The goal of this mechanism is to ensure the sustainability of these structures.

title: Apply for SLINGSHOT 2026 ($2M)
summary: SLINGSHOT 2026 invites deep tech startups globally to compete for over S$2.2 million in grant prizes and access to Singapore's innovation ecosystem. Applications close June 30, 2026.
content: SLINGSHOT 2026 is redefining the future as Singapore celebrates a decade of making the global homeground for innovation. The program invites deep tech startups with trailblazing innovations to compete and scale from Singapore's innovation arena – where you can experiment boldly, test fearlessly, and propel your business into the global spotlight. Take your startup to new heights by competing for a spot as one of SLINGSHOT 2026's Top 50 Global Startups! Gain access to Singapore's vibrant startup ecosystem and vie for a share of over S$2.2 million in grant prizes. Thematic Areas: Advanced Computing and Intelligence, Advanced Manufacturing and Industrial Systems, Digital Technologies and Securities, Environmental, Energy and Sustainability, Health and Biomedical. Benefits of Participation: Prize Grand Winner of SLINGSHOT 2025: S$400,000 Startup SG Grant Prize; 1st Runner Up: S$300,000 Startup SG Grant Prize; 2nd Runner Up: S$200,000 Startup SG Grant Prize. How to Apply: Ready to make Singapore your launchpad to the world? Click the SLINGSHOT 2026 portal to begin your application. The deadline for submission is Tuesday, June 30, 2026.

title: African Builder? Apply for Laptop LLM Challenge 2026 ($20k)
summary: Africa Deep Tech Challenge launches Laptop LLM Challenge 2026 with a $20k prize pool for innovators building efficient large language models that run on consumer-grade laptops, focusing on African innovation and accessible AI.
content: Applications are now open for the Laptop LLM Challenge 2026, an initiative by the Africa Deep Tech Challenge that invites innovators, researchers, developers, and AI builders to create large language model (LLM) solutions that run efficiently on consumer-grade laptops. The challenge seeks practical approaches to developing and deploying AI models that can operate with limited computing resources while addressing real-world problems across Africa. By encouraging locally relevant AI innovation, the programme aims to support the development of accessible language technologies that can function without dependence on expensive infrastructure. Benefits of Participation: - Cash Prize Opportunities: Selected teams will compete for a share of the competition's prize pool, with top-performing solutions receiving financial rewards. - Global Recognition: Participants will gain visibility among AI researchers, technology leaders, investors, and industry stakeholders. - Opportunity to Showcase Technical Expertise: The challenge provides a platform to demonstrate ability to build resource-efficient language models. - Contribution to African AI Development: Participants will develop solutions that address local challenges while contributing to Africa's AI ecosystem. - Community and Ecosystem Engagement: The programme brings together researchers, developers, startups, students, and AI practitioners for collaboration and partnerships. Eligibility: Open to individuals and teams with technical and research talent, with a focus on African innovation. How to Apply: Applications close on Friday, July 24, 2026. Participants are encouraged to submit entries before the deadline and ensure solutions meet all technical and documentation requirements.
